title: AI Is Changing How Creators Work And Earn
text:
Innovation
AI
AI Is Changing How Creators Work And Earn
By
Kolawole Samuel Adebayo
,
Contributor.
Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
I write about the economics of AI.
Follow Author
Dec 22, 2025, 06:25am EST
Dec 22, 2025, 12:16pm EST
From editing to monetization, AI is changing how creators work, earn and compete as platforms and audiences reshape the rules.
getty
When people talk about the
creator economy
today, they’re referring to one of the fastest-growing parts of the digital world. Individuals from every continent now make their living online by producing videos, writing newsletters, selling courses and hosting communities. What once looked like a side hustle for a few internet stars has turned into a global business.
The global creator economy has expanded quickly in recent years. Some estimates put its
market size
at about $203.6 billion in 2024, with projections suggesting it could grow far beyond that over the next decade. Much of that momentum is being fueled by new monetization models and the rapid spread of artificial intelligence tools that make content easier to produce and distribute.
AI is now woven into almost every part of the creator workflow. Tools that edit videos, generate captions, translate content or repurpose long videos into short clips are widely available. Roughly
86% of global content creators
already use generative AI in some form, and many say it helps them create content they would not have been able to produce on their own, according to recent survey by Adobe.
But as these tools become cheaper and more powerful, a bigger challenge is emerging: When everyone has access to the same technology, who actually benefits? The pressure to scale is real, but so is the risk of becoming interchangeable. What is unfolding is not just a shift in tools but a deeper change in power, ownership and the meaning of creative work itself.
The Big Numbers
Even with the trillion-dollar projections in the global creator economy, the momentum for growth hasn’t slowed at all. What’s changing now isn’t just how much content is being made, but where the money is flowing. Brands are no longer treating creators as an experimental channel; investment in creator marketing budgets is surging. In 2025, one industry
report
found that spending on creator partnerships jumped 171% year over year, as companies shifted budgets away from traditional ads and toward creator-led distribution. Companies increasingly view creator channels as essential media infrastructure, not as a mere side bet.
MORE FOR YOU
That shift is already visible in advertising data. In the U.S., the Interactive Advertising Bureau
reports
that ad spending tied to creators is now growing faster than overall media industry spending, a sign that attention and trust are moving in the same direction as dollars.
But advertising is only part of the story. Independent monetization — through subscriptions, memberships and gated communities — is becoming a more stable income source for many creators, especially those who control their audience relationships directly. Instead of relying on unpredictable algorithms, creators are increasingly building businesses around recurring revenue and owned channels.
Taken together, these shifts all point to a sharp inflection point in the creator economy: A rising number of people are now treating digital creation as their primary work, not a side hustle. While AI may be accelerating production, the bigger change is mostly economic: Who gets paid, how reliably and who controls the relationship between creators and their audience.
AI As A Workflow Amplifier
“AI has quickly become one of the most powerful scaling tools in a creator’s arsenal,” says Nikita Savrov, cofounder, CTO and CPO at
Uscreen
, a platform that helps creators build membership businesses.
In Savrov’s view, AI is not a creative replacement but a workflow amplifier. Tasks that once consumed hours, such as reformatting videos for different platforms, adding subtitles or organizing content libraries, can now be completed in a fraction of the time. For solo creators and small teams, that time savings can be transformative.
But speed can also be misleading. Savrov warns about what he calls “an illusion of productivity.” When AI makes it easier to publish more content, creators can feel like they are accomplishing more. Without a strategy, however, they are often just producing faster rather than better. “Volume does not equal value,” he told me.
Audience behavior appears to reflect that tension. In one
survey
, over half of consumers said they preferred no AI involvement in creative work, and nearly a third said it made them trust content less. Other studies show audiences remain open to AI-assisted content as long as it supports a human point of view rather than replacing it. The takeaway is simple: AI can streamline the work, but meaning still comes from people.
The New Battleground: Control And Ownership
For years, creators chased visibility. Now they’re chasing control — and Savrov sees a clear split brewing.
“Creators who use AI to build direct channels through email, communities or owned platforms are moving toward autonomy,” he explained. “Those who rely on AI purely to optimize for platform metrics are reinforcing their dependence.”
That distinction matters now more than ever. Platforms, from TikTok to Instagram to YouTube and more, are tightening control over data, monetization and distribution. Many now use AI to shape what users see — and by extension, who gets paid. Meanwhile, the creators investing in their own infrastructure — email lists, private communities and standalone apps — are also building insulation from sudden algorithm changes.
The idea of “owning your audience” isn’t just a talking point. It’s a business model shift. And AI, used intentionally, can help strengthen those direct ties. Used blindly, it makes creators more vulnerable.
What The Future Holds
AI is making it easier than ever to become a creator, and that number is growing fast. Some forecasts predict the number of global creators could hit
1 billion by 2032
, fueled by AI’s ability to reduce the need for production teams or technical skills.
But scale without identity doesn’t go very far. What’s becoming clear is that the creators who succeed over time will be the ones who connect with others the deepest and who use AI to support that connection, rather than primarily focusing on automation.
“Success still depends on the same things it always has,” Savrov noted. “Ideas, voice and the discipline to keep showing up.”
In other words, the tools may change or evolve, but the reasons people follow, listen or buy have not.
